revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Danpusan Kofun]][[fr:Kofun de Danpusan]][[ja:断夫山古墳]]
{{Short description|Ancient burial ground in Atsuta-ku, Nagoya, Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Dampusan Kofun | native_name = 断夫山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Atsuta-Jingu-Park.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Dampusan Kofun with Atsuta Jingu Park | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Dampusan Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.13083333|N|136.90305556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|en|Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|qid=Q1355625|lt=Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|1=Atsuta-ku, Nagoya}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|qid=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = No | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]|qq=}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Dampusan Kofun'''|断夫山古墳||qq=}} is a large keyhole-shaped ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located within the grounds of the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] complex in the Hayata neighborhood of {{ill|Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|en|Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|qid=Q1355625|lt=Atsuta-ku, Nagoya|1=Atsuta-ku, Nagoya}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1987.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138747 |title=断夫山古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020|qq=}}</ref> Per legend, the tumulus is the tomb is [[Miyazu-hime|Miyazuhime]], the wife of Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}, whose purported grave is located nearby.<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404|qq=}}{{in lang|ja|qq=}}</ref> ==Overview== The largest {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} in the Tōkai region of Japan, the Dampusan Kofun is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The tumulus has a total length of 151 meters, with the posterior circular portion containing the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] having a diameter of 80 meters and a height of 13 meters. The anterior rectangular portion has a width of dd6 meters and height of 16.2 meters. The posterior circular portion is actually thought to have been originally trapezoidal, and built in three tiers like a stepped pyramid, with lines of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' on each tier. The presence of a large number of rounded river boulders indicates that the surface may have originally been paved with stones (''[[fukiishi]]''). In addition, a square platform was built between the front and rear portions, which was possibly a stage for use in ceremonies. Numerous fragments of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|qid=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery have been recovered from this area. The tomb was originally surrounded by at least one moat which can clearly been seen in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} maps, but the urban encroachment of the surrounding area in the post-World War II era has obliterated most traces.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404|qq=}}{{in lang|ja|qq=}}</ref> On the tumulus, a row of very large cylindrical ''haniwa'' were recovered from the lower part of the posterior circular portion, along with a small amount of morning glory-shaped and figurative ''haniwa''. The cylindrical ''haniwa'' exhibit traces of both {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|qid=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} qualities. Some of the excavated ''haniwa'' are displayed at the {{ill|Nagoya City Museum|en|Nagoya City Museum|qid=Q10990728|lt=Nagoya City Museum|1=Nagoya City Museum}}. In addition, according to historical records, a large amount of Sue pottery was excavated from the tumulus, but most are now missing, and all of the pieces that have been handed down to the present are small pieces of unknown shape, which are stored in the Toyota City Museum of Local History and the Nagoya City Miharashidai Archaeological Museum. The interior of the Kofun has not been [[:en:Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] by modern methods. The name or Rank of the person buried in the tomb is unknown. Per legend, the tumulus is the tomb of Miyazuhime, the wife of Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}, whose purported grave is located nearby. Another theory is that the tomb is that of a powerful chieftain of the [[Owari clan]], perhaps Owari no Kusaka, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|qid=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}. From its construction method and the recovered artifacts, the tumulus is believed to date from the early 6th century AD, and to have had a strong connection to the Yamato kingdom. Many of these artifacts are currently preserved at the Anthropological Museum of {{ill|Nanzan University|en|Nanzan University|qid=Q1123198|lt=Nanzan University|1=Nanzan University}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The Dampusan Kofun was under the management of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] until World War II. According to an official cadastral map attached to a land register in 1885, the anterior portion is almost preserved, but one-third of the northwest part of the posterior circular portion is missing along with the moat. It seems that this area was used as a field. The current shape was restored when the ''kofun'' was designated as a historic site, and there is doubt as to whether the restoration is accurate. Likewise, the moat now surrounding the tumulus was a restoration attempted based on the 1885 map, and the actual moat had a width much larger than the modern restoration. Based on what is now known of the original moat dimensions, the actual length of the tumulus can be estimated at 160 meters. In addition, most major ''kofun'' in this region had double moats, and there is some evidence that a second shield-shaped moat once existed outside the current inner moat. After the war, ''kofun'' came under the control of {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|qid=Q11751|lt=Nagoya city|1=Nagoya}} until 1980, when it was taken over by the {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefectural government|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. it is about a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Jingunishi Station|Jingunishi Station]] on the {{ill|Nagoya Municipal Subway|en|Nagoya Municipal Subway|qid=Q1144874|lt=Nagoya Municipal Subway|1=Nagoya Municipal Subway}} [[:en:Meijo Line|Meijo Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/> ;Total length: 151 meters (estimated restoration of about 160 meters): ;Anterior rectangular portion: dd6 meters wide. 16 meters high, 3-tier ;Constriction width: 25 x 17 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 80 meter diameter x 13 meters high, 3-tiers ==Gallery== <gallery> CG Danpusan Kofun from above Atsuta-jingu Shrine 7p.JPG|Danpusan Kofun drawn in [[:en:3DCG|3DCG]] Danpusan-kofun 04.JPG|Panoramic view 高蔵遺跡出土 大型円筒埴輪 (名古屋市見晴台考古資料館).JPG|Haniwa excavated from Danpusan Kofun </gallery> ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|qid=Q9652090|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)}} *[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] ==References== {{reflist|qq=}} ==External links== {{commons Category:dd]] {{template-inline|qq=}} *[http://www.atsutajingu-park.com/enjoy/danpusan/index.html Atsuta Jingu park home page] {{in lang|ja|qq=}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/kinenbutu/siseki/kunisitei/0878.html Aichi Cultural Properties Navi]{{in lang|ja|qq=}} {{Atsuta Faith|qq=}}
{{Kofun navbar|qq=}}
[[file:断夫山古墳 周濠復原図.png|thumb|right|250px|Sketch of Danpusan Kofun]]
